coredata.dc:title: Green nanoarchitectonics of carbon quantum dots from Cinchona Pubescens Vahl as targeted and controlled drug cancer nanocarrier
coredata.dc:creator: González-Reyna, M.A.
Molina, Gustavo Andrés
Juarez-Moreno, Karla
Rodríguez-Torres, Angelina
Esparza, Rodrigo
Estevez, Miriam
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new carbon-based nanomaterial that has attracted tremendous attention due to their excellent fluorescent properties, chemical stability, water solubility, and biocompatibility features. Here, fluorescent CQDs synthesized by a green nanoarchitectonic method using Cinchona Pubescens Vahl extract were evaluated as drug nanocarriers for carboplatin (CBP) delivery. The characterization methods showed CQDs with semispherical shapes and sizes around 5 nm, temperature- and pH-dependent functional groups that interact with the CBP molecule adding specificity to the drug-delivery system. Based on the load efficiency results, it seems that the CQDs can carry almost 100 μg of carboplatin for every 1 mg of CQDs. This is possible due to the self-assembly process that takes place through the interaction between the protonation/deprotonation functional groups of CQDs and the hydrolyzed CBP molecule. Through this process, it is created spherical nanoparticles with an average size of 77.44 nm. The CQDs-CBP nanoparticles release the drug through a diffusion-controlled release mechanism where the acidic media is preferred, and the EPR effect also plays a helpful role. Besides, the viability test shows that the CQDs have almost null cytotoxicity suggesting that they could be used as a promising cancer treatment, improving the efficiency of cell internalization and significantly increasing their drug delivery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Drug delivery
Carboplatin drug
Carbon quantum dots
Hydrothermal synthesis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Dirección General de Asuntos del Personal Académico, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to the Laboratorio Universitario de Microscopia Electrónica of Instituto de Investigación de Materiales, to Alicia del Real for their technical support. M.A. González-Reyna would like to thank the “Dirección General de Asuntos del Personal Académico (DGAPA)” from the Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM) for her postdoctoral scholarship in the period between September 2021-August 2022 and to CONAHCYT for her postdoctoral scholarship in the period between October 2022-September 2023.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the synthetic route to obtain CQDs.

a) CQDs as synthesized under natural and UV light showing blue fluorescence, b) UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs, c) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs vs. concentration, and d) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs vs. pH.
a) FTIR of the CQDs, CBP, and CQDs-CBP spectra b) Representation of the structure of CQDs and CBP.
DLS analysis of a) CQDs through five months, b) CQDs-CBP nanocarrier in five days.
a) Low magnification BFSTEM image of CQDs, b) and c) high-resolution BFSTEM images of CQDs.
a) Low magnification BFSTEM image of CQDs-CBP, b) BFSTEM image of CQDs-CBP, c) High-resolution BFSTEM image of CQDs-CBP, and d) High-resolution HAADF-STEM image of CQDs-CBP.
Released profiles of CQDs against pH and temperature.
Cell viability of Cinchona Pubescens Vahl aqueous extract, the synthesized CQDs, the free CBP, and the complex CQDs-CBP on 3 T3-L1, HT-29, and MCF-7 cell lines.
ROS production of Cinchona Pubescens Vahl aqueous extract, the synthesized CQDs, the free CBP, and the complex CQDs-CBP on 3 T3-L1, HT-29, and MCF-7 cell lines.
DLE and DLC comparative using different nanocarriers and anticancer agents.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Kinetic parameters by the various pharmacokinetic models (k0, k1, ks, kH, and n) and their correlation coefficients.

head.ce:title: Green nanoarchitectonics of carbon quantum dots from Cinchona Pubescens Vahl as targeted and controlled drug cancer nanocarrier

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centro de Física Aplicada y Tecnología Avanzada, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Boulevard Juriquilla 3001, Querétaro 76230, Mexico.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centro de Física Aplicada y Tecnología Avanzada

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centro de Física Aplicada y Tecnología Avanzada. Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México. Boulevard Juriquilla 3001, Querétaro 76230, Mexico.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Facultad de Ciencias Naturales, Universidad Autónoma de Querétaro, Centro Universitario, Querétaro 76010, Mexico
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Facultad de Ciencias Naturales, Universidad Autónoma de Querétaro, Centro Universitario, Querétaro 76010, Mexico

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new carbon-based nanomaterial that has attracted tremendous attention due to their excellent fluorescent properties, chemical stability, water solubility, and biocompatibility features. Here, fluorescent CQDs synthesized by a green nanoarchitectonic method using Cinchona Pubescens Vahl extract were evaluated as drug nanocarriers for carboplatin (CBP) delivery. The characterization methods showed CQDs with semispherical shapes and sizes around 5 nm, temperature- and pH-dependent functional groups that interact with the CBP molecule adding specificity to the drug-delivery system. Based on the load efficiency results, it seems that the CQDs can carry almost 100 μg of carboplatin for every 1 mg of CQDs. This is possible due to the self-assembly process that takes place through the interaction between the protonation/deprotonation functional groups of CQDs and the hydrolyzed CBP molecule. Through this process, it is created spherical nanoparticles with an average size of 77.44 nm. The CQDs-CBP nanoparticles release the drug through a diffusion-controlled release mechanism where the acidic media is preferred, and the EPR effect also plays a helpful role. Besides, the viability test shows that the CQDs have almost null cytotoxicity suggesting that they could be used as a promising cancer treatment, improving the efficiency of cell internalization and significantly increasing their drug delivery.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemotherapy is the most common and effective approach for treating different types of cancer, which may be used alone or combined with other forms of therapy. Among all chemotherapy drugs, platinum anticancer drugs, such as cisplatin, carboplatin (CBP), and oxaliplatin, are widely used in clinical treatment because of their curative effect and well-known mechanism of action [1]. Nevertheless, their use is restricted due to severe adverse effects like high protein binding rate, cross-resistance, several toxic side effects, and lack of specificity [2]. Therefore, lipid-based [3], polymer-based [4], metal-based [5], and carbon-based [6] nanomaterials have been developed as drug delivery systems to overcome those problems.

Among them, all sp2 carbon-based nanomaterials, such as graphene, fullerene, and carbon nanotube (CNT), attract widespread attention for their promising performance in the biomedical field. Even more, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a promising member of the carbon nanomaterial family, with diameters between 1 nm and 10 nm, good water solubility, and tunable fluorescence properties, have emerged for simultaneous bioimaging and drug delivery. CQDs can be synthesized by different methods categorized as ‘top-down’ and ‘bottom-up’ [7]. In particular, hydrothermal treatment, a bottom-up method, is commonly used because it is cost-effective and eco-friendly. This method uses different carbon sources like citric acid [8] or natural sources like apple juice [9] that react at high pressure and temperature inside an autoclave. Besides, considering that the use of hetero atoms like nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus improves the properties of the CQDs, the use of natural sources like plants adds value to the hydrothermal treatment [10], becoming it into a green nanoarchitectonic process. Nanoarchitectonics involves merging various unit processes, like molecular synthesis, atom and molecular manipulation, self-assembly, field-applied assembly, micro-fabrication, and bio-related processes, to form functional materials [11]. This technique relies on the interactions between materials/molecules at the nanoscale, where specific physicochemical features play a crucial role in material creation [11]. For instance, a recent article by Rao and Pramoda [12] discussed how altering the atomic compositional arrangements in two-dimensional and related materials can change their properties. They found that introducing heteroatoms like nitrogen and boron into graphene through chemical doping can regulate the band gaps and produce beneficial properties, producing compositionally architected materials useful in various energy-related applications. On the other hand, to improve drug delivery, researchers have customized nanoparticles to ensure targeted and sustained release of drugs, genes, and proteins [13]. These materials are also designed to respond to specific stimuli, such as changes in temperature, pH, or magnetic forces, allowing for controlled administration of therapeutics [13].

Even when all carbon nanomaterials have been used for drug delivery, especially for carrying doxorubicin [14–16], there are fewer examples of the delivery of platinum anticancer drugs, even when they have shown promising results. For instance, Liying Wei et al. [2] showed graphene oxide modified with a series of high polymers and folic acid to effectively deliver cisplatin, oxaliplatin, eptaplatin, and CBP with higher cumulative release rates in an acid environment than in neutral. They also showed that those drug delivery systems could inhibit the growth of human ovarian cancer cells (SKOV3). Also, Leyva-González et al. [17] led the development of a nanovector generated with commercial CNTs chemically functionalized with hyaluronic acid and loaded with CBP. Their CNTs were evaluated in human cervical adenocarcinoma (HeLa) and mammary adenocarcinoma (MDA-MB-231), demonstrating a specific in vitro dose-dependent antitumor effect.

Here, CBP was chosen because, to our knowledge, no one has yet reported the use of CQDs for delivering CBP, even when CQDs are known for their high biocompatibility and low toxicity. The present work synthesized CQDs by hydrothermal method from Cinchona Pubescens Vahl, which produces CQDs with different oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino, etc.), allowing a high-water solubility and offering anchor sites to load a drug. The CQDs were characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), zeta potential, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), and UV–vis absorption spectra (UV–vis) to understand their particle morphology, hydrodynamic diameter, surface charge, chemical composition, and spectral properties. The drug delivery system was analyzed to evaluate their drug-loaded efficiency and release efficiency against temperature and pH, making a preliminary study to provide a basis for further application of low cytotoxic CBP nanocarrier with specific targeting to cancer cell lines.

All chemicals and reagents were p.a. grade, purchased from Sigma Aldrich, and used as received without further purification. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) was prepared using 137 mM NaCl, 2.7 mM KCl, 10 mM Na2HPO4, and 1.8 mM KH2PO4 dissolved in distilled water. The barks of Cinchona Pubescens Vahl were obtained from the local market “General Mariano Escobedo” in Santiago de Querétaro, Querétaro, México. Samples were identified according to the guidelines published by the National Herbarium (Instituto de Biología, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, México) under registration code IBUNAM:MEXU:93414 [1]. A carboplatin injectable solution (CBP) was brought from ULSA TECH pharmaceutical for the CBP loading and release tests. Human colorectal adenocarcinoma HT-29 cell line (HTB-38), human breast adenocarcinoma MCF-7 cells (HTB-22), and murine fibroblasts 3 T3-L1 (CL-173) were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC). All cell lines were grown in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) supplemented with fetal bovine serum at 10 % v/v, 1 % l-glutamine, and 1 % antibiotic and antimycotic. Cell lines were cultivated at 37 °C and 5 % CO2 atmosphere until 80–90 % confluency for their usage.

Following the synthesis illustrated in Fig. 1 , CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment using a Cinchona Pubescens Vahl aqueous extract. First, 5 g of the prepared Cinchona Pubescens Vahl sample was added to 50 mL of H2O for a maceration extraction at room temperature for 24 h. Then the mixture was filtered, transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, and heated at 200 °C for 4 h. The resultant solution was dialyzed against water for 48 h. Finally, it was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C and re-dispersed in distilled water at 1 mg/mL.

The morphological and structural characterization of the QDs and QDs-CP were analyzed using a Hitachi cold-field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM/STEM) (Hitachi High-Tech Co., Tokyo, Japan) and a Jeol JEM-ARM200F scanning transmission electron microscope (TEM/STEM) (JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). Functional groups were determined by FTIR analysis, recorded from 450 to 4000 cm−1, using a Perkin Elmer Spectrum Two FT-IR spectrometer with a universal ATR accessory with a diamond crystal. The absorption spectra were measured by a Perkin Elmer UV–vis lambda 365 spectrophotometer. An Anton Paar Litesizer 500 DLS analyzer was used for particle size and zeta potential measurements. The temperature was fixed at 25 °C, the equilibrium time at two minutes, and glass cells were used. For particle size, the optimum angle was automatically found for each sample based on transmittance. At the same time, the zeta potential was taken using the univet cell accessory and calculated through the Smoluchowski approximation.

2 mg of CBP was added to 20 mL of CQDs (1 mg/mL). Magnetic stirring was conducted at ambient temperature for 24 h to ensure equilibrium adsorption. The resulting mixture was dialyzed for 1 h to remove unreacted CBP and labeled as CQD-CBP.

The amount of CBP was calculated by the absorbance of CBP at 230 nm using a standard calibration curve. The drug loading efficiency (DLE) and loading content (DLC) were both calculated according to the following experimental equations:
(1)
DLE
%
=
Amount of
CBP
loaded in
CDs
Initial amount of
CBP
× 100 %
(2)
DLC
mg
g
− 1
=
Amount of
CBP
loaded in
CDs
Initial amount of
CDs

CBP release study was conducted using PBS solutions with pH 6.0 and 7.4 at 37 °C to investigate the pH-dependent release property. The buffer (1 mL) was taken out at different time intervals, and 1 mL of fresh PBS with the same pH was added to the dialysis medium to maintain the pH. Then, the abovementioned method was used to quantify the amount of released CBP.

Diverse pharmacokinetic models were fitted for the drug release profiles to get insight into the release mechanism of the CQD-CBP system. First, the Donbrow-Samuelov zero-order kinetics (Eq. (3)) for prolonged pharmaceutical action was used, where
Q 0
is the initial amount of drug in the solution,
Q t
is the amount of drug dissolved at time t , and
k 0
is the zero-release constant.
(3)
Q t
=
Q 0
+
k 0
t

Next, the Gibladi-Feldman-Wagner first-order kinetics (Eq. (4)) for proportional drug release against time was fitted, and
k 1
is the first-release rate constant.
(4)
log
Q t
= log
Q 0
−
k 1
2.303
t

Then, the Higuchi model (Eq. (5)) for square root time-dependent drug release based on a diffusion process that follows Fick's law was tested, where
k H
is the Higuchi rate or dissolution constant.
(5)
Q t
=
k H
t
1 / 2

Additionally, the Korsmeyer-Peppas model (Eq. (6)) for diverse pharmaceutical modified release dosage forms describing an exponential drug release against a determined elapsed time was used, where a is a constant that incorporated structural-geometrical properties of the pharmaceutical vehicle,
f t
is the fractional release of the drug, and n is the release exponent that describes the mechanism of diffusion.
(6)
f t
= a
t n

Finally, the Baker and Lonsdale drug release model was used since it describes the controlled drug release from different spherical matrices such as micro and nanoparticles formulations. It is described in Eq. (7) where
M t
is the amount of the drug released at the time t,
M ∞
is the amount released at the infinite time and
K LB
is the release constant.
(7)
K LB
t =
3 2
1 −
1 −
M t
M ∞
2 / 3
−
M t
M ∞
In all cases, the Leverberg-Marquardt algorithm (LM) has been used to fit the data of the release profiles.

A colorimetric assay based on the reduction of 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2H- tetrazolium bromide (MTT) was performed to assess cell viability as follows: 10,000 cells per well were seeded in a 96-well plate for 24 h, at 37 °C and 5 % in a CO2 atmosphere.

Then, cells were exposed to different concentrations of CQDs, CQDs loaded with the drug, free drug, and extract, in a final volume of 100 μL of DMEM. After this, cells were incubated with the treatments for 48 h at 37 °C and in a 5 % CO2 atmosphere. Then, cells were rinsed three times with PBS, and 10 μL of MTT and 90 μL of DMEM were added to each well and incubated for 4 h at 37 °C and 5 % in a CO2 atmosphere. After this, 100 μL of isopropanol was added to each well, the plate was read at 570 nm, and background absorbance was read at 690 nm. Positive control was assessed by incubating the cells with Tween 20 at 1 % and was used to calculate cell viability.

The detection of reactive oxygen species (ROS) was assessed with 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DFCDA) as follows: cells were incubated with different concentrations of treatments for 48 h; afterward, cells were rinsed with PBS and incubated for 90 min with 30 μM of DFCDA at 37 °C and 5 % in a CO2 atmosphere. Then, the fluorescence of treated cells was detected with an excitation wavelength of 485 nm and an excitation wavelength of 530 nm. The intrinsic amount of ROS was assessed by incubating the cells exclusively with DMEM. All experiments were performed in triplicates.

After the synthesis method, the CQDs emitted blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light (365 nm) (Fig. 2a). Thus, UV–Vis and photoluminescence spectrophotometry were employed to study the optical characteristics of CQDs. First, as shown in Fig. 2b, CQDs reveal intense optical absorbance from 210 to 320 nm, and their absorption intensity presents concentration dependency consistent with the behavior of different materials in the bibliography [18]. Given the structure of CQDs, the absorption peak centered around 230 nm is caused by the π–π* transition of CC bonds comprised of sp2 and sp3 carbon atoms. In contrast, the rest of the spectra represent transitions n-π*, originating from backbone chains or functional groups attached to the surface of CQDs.

Then, the emission properties of the CQDs were analyzed, showing that the fluorescence is highly influenced by concentration (Fig. 2c) and pH (Fig. 2d). Here, surface state phenomena are considered one of the different mechanisms that give the origin of the fluorescence of CQDs [19]. Therefore, higher oxygen-containing groups and surface defects will generate more intense fluorescence at higher concentrations of CQDs, while the surface functional group protonation and deprotonation cause pH-dependent emission that changes the intensity and color of the emitted fluorescence (Fig. 2d) [19,20].

The presence of functional groups that are easy to protonate and deprotonate suggests that CQDs also exhibit remarkable sensing features and make them convenient for being functionalized with drugs. FTIR spectroscopy was employed to identify those functional groups. Likewise, to understand the CQDs loading process, CBP was also analyzed before and after being added to the CQDs (Fig. 3 ).

The spectra of the CQDs show a broad absorption band between 3700 cm−1 and 2000 cm−1, caused for the contribution of hydrate (H2O), hydroxyl (-OH), ammonium, or amino groups, which also allow excellent solubility in aqueous solutions [21]. CQDs are soluble in different solvents due to their chemical surface comprising hydrophilic, hydrophobic, or amphiphilic functional groups [22]. Nevertheless, most of the reviewed literature studies usually do not quantify the dissolution rate of the CQDs, even when it is an essential factor for different types of applications. Then, the solubility in water of the synthesized CQDs was evaluated, showing a dissolution rate of 30 mg per 1 mL of water. To our knowledge, only Allam et al. [23] quantified the dissolution rate of their CQDs, isolated from carbon soot, also showing 30 mg/mL. The CQDs also showed a sharp peak at about 1700 cm−1, attributed to a carbonyl double bond, and an intense band at about 1600 cm−1 from CC bonds.

On the other hand, the spectrum of CBP is quite different. It shows a band associated with an NH group (3270 cm−1) followed by a band between 2958 and 2862 cm−1, corresponding to asymmetric and symmetric CH stretching vibrations. At lower wavenumbers appear peaks attributed to esther groups (CO at 1635 cm−1 and CO at 1348 cm−1) and the bending vibrations of NH and NH2 at 1605 and 1458 cm−1, respectively. Finally, the characteristic IR peaks for the Pt-NH2 bond appear at 1380 and 1260 cm−1.

The spectra of the CQDs-CBP sample also showed a broad band composed of the typical band of an NH stretch around 3490–3430 cm−1, caused by the presence of the amino groups, and a narrow band around 3000 cm−1 caused by CH bonds. This spectrum is an effect of the superposition of peaks of CBP and CQDs. However, the fact that the complex made by the CQDs and the CBP shows a thinner band related to H bonds implies two things; first, CBP could be loaded on the CQDs by hydrogen bonds, allowing a higher release rate at higher temperatures. Furthermore, functional groups such as amine, imidazolyl, and carboxyl on the surface of CQDs are characterized by their easy protonation under acidic conditions [24], suggesting that when the complex CQDs-CBP reach acidic target sites, they will rapidly release the drug contained. Second, the complex CQDs-CBP has lower water solubility than the CQDs and higher than the CBP, probably causing some agglomeration comparing them with the CQDs alone but suggesting that the complex will interact better with cells than free CBP.

The next step in the characterization was DLS. DLS is a technique commonly used to analyze dispersing colloidal samples. It offers information about nanoparticle sizes, stability, and aggregation or agglomeration. This information is essential in biological applications because the aggregation of nanoparticles can alter experimental reproducibility by changing the cellular uptake of the particles and toxicity profile [25]. Therefore, to evaluate if the CQDs present changes in their colloidal properties, DLS analysis was made against time. As shown in Fig. 4 , the sample offers good stability, even after five months, where only slight variations are presented with a peak value from 4.54 nm to 6.28 nm. Here, interactions between functional groups and water could cause variations in the adsorbing layer of the surface of each monitored particle, increasing the diameter measured by DLS [26]. Additionally, the CQDs-CBP compound underwent DLS analysis for a shorter duration due to the delivery process relying on interactions with the media. As a result, the value change occurred more rapidly compared to the sample with only CQDs. Over a period of five days, the peak value decreased from 77.44 nm to 56 nm, indicating a partial delivery of CBP to the water and resulting in smaller nanoparticles.

Then, the morphology and the size of the samples were evaluated by combining scanning electron image (SEM) and transmission electron image (TEM). Fig. 5a shows a brig-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (BFSTEM) image of the CQDs obtained in the SEM/STEM microscope. The image shows that the CQDs are well-distributed and embedded in an organic matrix. High-resolution BFSTEM was performed on a TEM/STEM microscope to analyze the structure of the CQDs further. Fig. 5b shows that the CQDs are <5 nm in size. The interplanar distance measurement of 0.252 nm corresponds to the (100) plane of the graphene structure.

Fig. 6 shows the images obtained from CQDS-CBP. The images obtained with the BFSTEM at low magnification are shown in Fig. 6a and b. It can be seen that the CQDs tend to form agglomerates after the incorporation of CBP, forming groups with diameters <100 nm. It is suggested that when CQDs and CBP are in a water-based solution, they interact and form an aggregate. Initially, CBP undergoes hydrolysis through a biphasic mechanism that involves a ring-opening process followed by the loss of the malonate ligand [27]. Then, upon contact with the media, the resultant CBP molecule and CQDs experience protonation/deprotonation. Ultimately, the self-assembly of the CQDs-CBP complex generates spherical nanoparticles. Charge and intermolecular interactions, such as hydrogen bonds, regulate this process. Similar mechanisms have been reported for graphene oxide in aqueous solutions under different pH values and in the presence of divalent cations [28,29]. Therefore, the CQDs-CBP system also falls under the category of nanoarchitectonics, which involves using nanoscale units and principles of nanotechnology to architect functional materials [11].

Fig. 6c shows a high-resolution BFSTEM image where an amorphous-like structure is observed due to the particle thickness and the fact that CQDs are completely embedded in CBP. Fig. 6d shows a high-angle annular dark field (HAADF)-STEM image, where the contrast of the image is related to the atomic number of the sample. The image shows some bright spots related to Pt atoms that are well dispersed in the particle, so the CQDs do not change the structure of the system.

The surface charges (zeta potentials) were also determined, an essential parameter for long-term stability, giving −21.2 mV the sample of CQDs, −6.9 mV to the sample of pure CBP dissolved in water, and −22.5 mV to the complex CQDs-CBP. These values confirm that the loading process was made by chemical interactions, such as hydrogen bonds, and not electrostatic bonds, because both samples present a negative charge.

Considering that a common method to target cancer cells while sparing normal ones is to utilize leaky vasculatures caused by tumor growth. This allows drugs to reach the interstitium of tumor cells via newly produced blood vessels from the angiogenesis process. Due to a poorly organized lymphatic system in tumors, drugs can remain in the tumor interstitium once they gain access. This is called the enhanced permeability and retention effect (EPR). To effectively deliver drugs through EPR, using anionic or neutral nanocarriers with sizes ranging from 10 to 100 nm is recommended, as they can move into the tumor site through leaky vasculature and improve circulation half-life. This recommendation is based on the ability of the kidneys to filter particles smaller than 10 nm and positively charged particles. Since the CQDs-CBP complex has an average size of 77.44 nm and a negative surface charge, it is a good candidate for targeted delivery through the EPR effect.

The CQDs-CBP complex was found to have a loading efficiency of 96.5 % and release properties of 96.466 mg g−1. These values are higher than what is typically reported in the literature for using CQDs and doxorubicin [30–32]. They are even higher than the use of graphene oxide to load CBP [33], as seen in Table 1 . This exceptional loading behavior can be attributed to the proposed loading mechanism, where the highly functionalized surface of the synthesized CQDs interacts with the hydrolyzed CBP molecule, forming dense clusters. This is supported by the highest loading efficiency observed in CQDs functionalized with quinic acid to load gemcitabine [34] or in the scenario where carbon dots are functionalized with tryptophan prior to the introduction of the anticancer drug curcumin [35].

It was reported that the pH in the tumor tissue shows weak acidity (6.5–7.2), providing an ideal trigger for drug-controlled release. Based on this, the pH-dependent release behaviors of CQDs-CBP were examined in PBS buffer with different pH values.

Fig. 7 shows the complex CQDs-CBP drug release profile at different pH and temperatures. The three profiles have a similar tendency which can be divided into two phases. The first corresponds to the so-called burst release, while the other to a sustained release phase of CBP. The burst release phase might be produced by the poor interaction of some adsorbed drug with the surface of the carbon dots, which can be visualized during the first 6 h. The second phase has a plateau within the next 3 h up to 54 h, which validates a sustained release of the CBP from the CQDs as observed in other works (principally using doxorubicin) and can prevail up to 72 h [36–38]. The long time needed to observe the drug release profiles of CQD-CBP suggest its use as a pharmaceutical vehicle for sustained drug delivery systems.

In particular, the cumulative release property of CBP exhibited significant pH-dependent properties, where the CBP release rate in an acid environment was much higher than that at neutral. This could be due to pH-sensitive chemical bonding made when the CQDs were loaded with the CBP; these kinds of bonds are unstable and easy to dissociate in an acidic environment, allowing a pH-targeted influence when the CBP is delivered. Therefore, taking into account the morphology, surface charge and pH-dependence, the system presents doble target-delivery mechanism, the EPR effect and acid preferential delivery. Moreover, it was also evaluated that the impact of temperature showed similar rates to those at neutral pH. This temperature-dependence behavior could be triggered because the hydrogen bonding between CQDs and CBP becomes weak at higher temperatures, allowing the drug to release faster to the media.
Five kinetic models were used to understand the released pattern of the CQDs-CBP sample, where the fitting parameters were acquired, and their correlation coefficients can be observed in Table 2 .

From the pharmacokinetics results, it is observed that the release of CBP can be fitted using the Higuchi model for all samples conditions tested since it possesses correlation values (R2) of 0.93, 0.97, and 0.98 for pH 6 at 25 °C, pH 6 at 37 °C and pH 7 at 37 °C, respectively, explaining the slow release of the drug.

Overall, results show that the release mechanism is diffusion controlled, similar to those reported from other CQD-drugs systems such as minocycline [39], doxorubicin hydrochloride [40–43], metformin [44], mitomycin C [44], and cyclophosphamide [44]. This should be expected since the CQDs are inside a polymer dialysis tubing that restrains the release profile. Further confirmation of the diffusion model can be observed from the Korsmeyer-Peppas model, in which the release exponent n is determined. In all cases, n was found to be < 0.45 , characteristic of Fickian diffusion, but also for polydisperse systems [39] such as the QD with different sizes.

From the dissolution constants of the Higuchi model, it is observed that the highest rates are obtained at higher temperatures (37 °C). Also, comparing different pHs at this temperature, it is observed that release is faster at lower pH. This result suggests that under more acidic conditions, the -NH2 functional groups break their bonding with the carboxyl groups accelerating the amount of CBP diffused to the media, while the temperature causes weaker hydrogen bonds between the -NH3 of the CBP and hydroxyl/ carboxyl groups of CQDs that eventually also break.

The cytotoxicity of the Cinchona Pubescens Vahl aqueous extract, the synthesized CQDs, the free CBP, and the complex CQDs-CBP was measured by treating 3 T3-L1, HT-29, and MCF-7 cells (Fig. 8 ). For the viability tests, the free CBP concentrations were calculated by considering the loading value of the CQDs; therefore, it was analyzed at 10 % of the concentration of CQDs.

Looking at the HT-29 cells, the effect caused by the extract at concentrations below 100 μg/mL draws attention due to increased cell viability that does not happen against MCF-7 cells. This cannot be attributed to a proliferative effect. Different nutrients in the aqueous extract likely help early cell multiplication. As observed for all the cell lines tested, the extract does not show cytotoxic effects because the cell viability values were consistently higher than 90 %. The lack of cytotoxic effects is essential, considering its further use as a carbon source for CQDs synthesis. Therefore, after treating the three cell lines with CQDs, it was expected similar behaviors than when they were in contact with the extract. However, even when CQDs show a null effect against HT-29, the sample presents low toxicity against MCF-7 and 3 T3-L1 cells. Indeed, some biosynthetic CQDs have been proven to induce antiproliferative effects on MCF-7 human cancer cells [45]. In this way, at 25 μg/mL of CQDs, the cell viability is at 100 % and decreases to around 75 % after adding 50 μg/mL of CQDs but remains with cell viability between 70 and 65 % even at concentrations of 400 μg/mL of CQDs. These results fully confirm the low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility of CQDs [46]. As previously reported, CQDs were proven to be non-toxic to HeLa, 4 T1 and HepG2 cancer cells. Furthermore, they demonstrated effective targeting of the H22 tumor region following intravenous administration. The accumulation of CQDs in the tumor region was attributed to their small size, which enables them to take advantage of the EPR effect [47]. Then considering the characterization results, we can suggest the same mechanism effect in our system.

Meanwhile, the cytotoxicity of free CBP and CQDs-CBP complex was also examined. CBP shows the typical anticancer activity on both cancer cells, decreasing their viabilities as the free CBP concentration increases, causing cellular death to 50 % of the population since the drug gets 2.5 μg/mL of concentration, demonstrating the CBP's high toxicity. The action mechanism of CBP easily explains this result. CBP acts as a bifunctional alkylating agent that forms irreversible covalent bonds that inhibit DNA replication, RNA transcription, and protein synthesis, causing cell cycle arrest and then inducing programmed cell death or apoptosis. Thus, the cell survival rate persists at 50 % even at the highest concentration tested; those cells are under cell cycle arrest.

Finally, looking at the CQDs-CBP complex is evident the similarity in the tendency between the complex concentration and the free CBP. Nevertheless, the amount required to demise the cell population to 50 % is 75 μg/mL for the MCF-7 cell line, while for HT-29, the concentration must be raised to 300 μg/mL. Then in both cases, the survival rate is constant due to the cycle arrest. It's worth noting that there is a significant difference in the toxicity levels of CQDs and CBP. In fact, CQDs have almost no cytotoxicity, slightly higher vs. MCF-7, which means that the CBP plays a crucial role in determining how cells will react to the CQDs-CBP complex. To determine if the CQDs-CBP complex acts as a targeted drug nanocarrier that delivers CBP specifically to tumor cells, we conducted the same viability test on the non-tumor cell line 3 T3-L1. Sui et al. demonstrated that graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can boost the anticancer efficacy of cisplatin. This enhancement primarily occurs by increasing the cellular uptake of the drug [48]. Here, the extract shows a null cytotoxic effect again, while the CQDs present low cytotoxicity with a cell survival rate of 78 % at 400 μg/mL. On the contrary, CBP has a high mortality rate reaching around 50 % at 40 μg/mL. Interestingly, the complex has a significantly lower cytotoxic effect than the free CBP, maintaining the same survival rate as CQDs alone. In this way, the drug nanocarrier system could diminish side effects and improve cancer treatment efficiency.

The principal differences between the behavior of each cell line could also be related to their metabolic path. Overall, the results may indicate that the size advantage of CQDs makes them an excellent foundation for creating innovative tools to visualize their effect in the tumor microenvironment. This is due to their ability to leverage the EPR effect [49–51].

.Then, the overproduction of ROS was evaluated due to its linking to the cytotoxic effect of nanomaterials [52]. It has been reported that CQDs have dual functions of ROS generators and scavengers. However, it depends on several factors, such as their carbon core surface groups [53]. Thus, addressing their effect on ROS production is fundamental to complement their nanotoxicological evaluation. Even when all the samples presented an increase in ROS production (Fig. 9 ), the CQDs showed the lowest induction of ROS production in all the cell lines, while the free CBP showed the highest. Interestingly, even when it does not show any cytotoxic effect, the extract can induce an overproduction of ROS, reaching its highest level at 75 μg/mL on the three cell lines. CBP showed the maxima induction of ROS generation at 25 μg/mL for HT-29 and 3 T3-L1, while the value for the MCF-7 cell line increased at 50 μg/mL. Then, as the free CBP concentration increased, the oxidative stress decreased for the three cell lines because this concentration was closer to the IC50 value (i.e., fewer live cells were present to generate radicals). When the overproduction of ROS by the CQDs-CBP complex is evaluated, MCF-7 cells show more extensive ROS overproduction than HT-29 and 3 T3-L1. Then, MCF-7 mainly, and then HT-29 cells resulted more prone to the cytotoxic effects of CQDs-CBP than 3 T3-L1 cells.

CQDs were created using an extract of Cinchona Pubescens Vahl through a hydrothermal process. The CQDs obtained were analyzed using SEM, UV–vis, and FTIR to determine their morphology, spectral properties, and chemical composition. The results showed that the CQDs had a high surface area to volume ratio due to their small size, and they remained stable in colloid form for five months. Additionally, their water solubility was quantified, and it was found to be 30 mg/mL, a value that has only been reported once. The CQDs also had a negative surface potential due to functional groups such as hydroxy, carboxyl, and amino groups on their surface, making them suitable for functionalization with CBP, a platinum-based cancer drug.

The CQDs show a relatively high loading capacity and a good drug release profile. This is due to the loading mechanism suggested, which involves the highly functionalized surface of the CQDs synthesized, and the hydrolyzed CBP molecule. In an aqueous environment, protonation and deprotonation occur, followed by the formation of dense clusters of CQDs covered by CBP, facilitated by charge and intermolecular interactions.

Based on its negative surface charge and the average size of 77.44 nm, the CQDs-CBP complex shows promise for use in chemotherapeutic cancer applications. This is due to its ability to use the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect. In addition, the CQDs display pH-dependent release behavior activated by hydrogen bonds formed between their functional groups, the hydrolyzed CBP molecule, and the surrounding aqueous environment. Besides, the long time needed to observe the drug release profiles of CQDs-CBP suggest its use as a pharmaceutical vehicle for sustained and targeted drug delivery systems.

The biocompatibility of the system was evaluated against the 3 T3-L1, MCF-7, and HT-29 cell lines. The results showed that the CBP component played a significant role in how cells responded to the CQDs-CBP complex. MCF-7 cells had the highest level of cytotoxicity, followed by HT-29, while 3 T3-L1 cells showed reduced mortality. These findings suggest that the green nanoarchitectonic system has potential benefits for cancer treatment, with minimal adverse effects during chemotherapy treatment, thanks to its pH-dependent drug release behavior and the EPR effect.

M.A. González-Reyna: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Gustavo Andrés Molina: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Karla Juarez-Moreno: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Angelina Rodríguez-Torres: Resources. Rodrigo Esparza: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Miriam Estevez: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the Laboratorio Universitario de Microscopia Electrónica of Instituto de Investigación de Materiales, to Alicia del Real for their technical support. M.A. González-Reyna would like to thank the “Dirección General de Asuntos del Personal Académico (DGAPA)” from the Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM) for her postdoctoral scholarship in the period between September 2021-August 2022 and to CONAHCYT for her postdoctoral scholarship in the period between October 2022-September 2023.

Das et al., “Biocompatible carbon dots derived from κ-carrageenan and phenyl boronic acid for dual modality sensing platform of sugar and its anti-diabetic drug release behavior,” Int J Biol Macromol, vol. 132, pp. 316–329, 2019, doi:10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2019.03.224.
